# Hardcoded Version vs Platform
*or what to Do When a Single Declaration Escapes the Platform*

This scenario demonstrates that an explicit version overrides a `platform()` recommendation.

#### Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] -- platform --> BOM[demo-bom:1.0.0]
    Root --> LibA[lib-a:2.0.0]
```

#### Gradle (Groovy DSL)

```{.groovy include="/demos/gradle-demo/scenario-4-override/build.gradle" snippet="s4-deps"}
```

#### Expected Intuition
"I typed 2.0.0, so I get 2.0.0."

#### Actual Resolution Results
Resolves `2.0.0`.

#### Classpath Reality
`2.0.0`.

#### Why This Happens
An explicit version is a stronger constraint than a platform recommendation (unless `enforcedPlatform` is used).

#### How Developers Commonly "Fix" This
N/A

#### Safer Ways to Take Control
Use `enforcedPlatform` if you want the BOM to win.

#### Signals to Watch For
N/A

#### How This Scales in Real Systems
Allows local overrides of global defaults.

#### Key Takeaway
Explicit versions override standard platforms.

#### Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 3: Mgmt](../scenario-3-mgmt/guide.qmd)
